concepts.lesson: People become a resource when investments are made in their education, training, and medical care. This investment transforms a human being into 'Human Capital,' which is the stock of skill and productive knowledge embodied in them.
concepts.real_example: Sakal's parents invested in his education and vocational training in computers, which led him to a high-paying job in a firm.
key_points: Education increases the efficiency and productivity of a person.
Investment in human capital yields a return just like physical capital.
Human resource is superior to other resources because it can make use of land and capital.

concepts.concept_name: Economic Activities by Men and Women
concepts.lesson: Activities are classified into 'Market Activities' (performed for pay or profit) and 'Non-Market Activities' (production for self-consumption). Historically, there is a division of labor where women generally look after domestic chores and men work in fields.
concepts.real_example: Vilas’s mother, Geeta, earned an income by selling fish (Market Activity), whereas Sakal’s mother, Sheela, looked after domestic chores (Non-Market Activity).
key_points: Market activities include remuneration to anyone who performs an activity.
Household work performed by women is not recognized in the National Income.
Education and skill are the major determinants of the earnings of any individual in the market.

concepts.lesson: The quality of the population depends upon the literacy rate, health of a person, and skill formation. Education opens new horizons, provides new aspirations, and develops values of life.
concepts.real_example: Government initiatives like 'Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan' aim to provide elementary education to all children aged 6–14 years.
key_points: Literacy rates have increased from 18% in 1951 to 74% in 2010-11.
There is a huge difference in literacy between males and females and between urban and rural areas.
Vocational education is being developed to provide occupation-related knowledge.

concepts.lesson: Health is an indispensable basis for realizing one's well-being. An unhealthy person becomes a liability for an organization. The National Health Policy aims at improving the accessibility of healthcare, family welfare, and nutritional service.
concepts.real_example: Improvement in life expectancy (up to 68.3 years in 2014) and reduction in Infant Mortality Rate (IMR).
key_points: A healthy person can realize their potential and be productive.
Infant Mortality Rate is the death of a child below one year of age.
There is still a vast difference in health infrastructure across different states in India.

concepts.lesson: In rural areas, unemployment is often 'Seasonal' (lack of work during non-harvest periods) or 'Disguised' (more people are working than required). In disguised unemployment, the marginal productivity of extra workers is zero.
concepts.real_example: A family of eight working on a small plot that only requires five people; the three extra people are 'disguisedly' unemployed.
key_points: Seasonal unemployment happens when people are unable to find jobs during some months of the year.
Disguised unemployment is a major problem in the agricultural sector.
It leads to a wastage of manpower resource.

concepts.lesson: A peculiar feature of urban areas is educated unemployment. Many youth with matriculation, graduation, and post-graduation degrees are not able to find jobs, leading to a 'paradoxical manpower situation.'
concepts.real_example: A surplus of manpower in certain categories (like general graduates) coexisting with a shortage of manpower in others (like technical skills).
key_points: Unemployment leads to a feeling of hopelessness and despair among the youth.
It increases the economic overload on the working population.
The quality of life of an individual as well as of society is adversely affected.
